title: Data Mesh
summary: Data Mesh principles, domain ownership of data products, self-serve data infrastructure, and federated governance.

sections.content:
## The Data Warehouse Problem

Traditional data architectures centralize data in a warehouse owned by a single data team. As the organization scales, this becomes a bottleneck:
- Central team becomes a bottleneck for all data needs
- Disconnected from domain knowledge (who understands order data? the orders team)
- Long lead times for data pipelines
- Quality issues accumulate

**Data Mesh** (Zhamak Dehghani, 2019) applies microservices thinking to data.

sections.title: Centralized Data Lake vs Data Mesh
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Centralized Data Lake
        DomainA[Order Domain] -->|ETL| DL[Central Data Lake]
        DomainB[User Domain] -->|ETL| DL
        DomainC[Product Domain] -->|ETL| DL
        DL --> Report[Reports & Analytics]
        Note[Central team owns everything\nBottleneck, context lost]
    end
    subgraph Data Mesh
        OrderDP[Orders Domain\nData Products]
        UserDP[Users Domain\nData Products]
        ProductDP[Products Domain\nData Products]
        OrderDP -->|API| Consumer[Data Consumer]
        UserDP -->|API| Consumer
        ProductDP -->|API| Consumer
    end
    style DL fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style Consumer fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Four Principles of Data Mesh

### 1. Domain Ownership
Data is owned by the domain that produces it. The Orders team owns and publishes order data products. They understand it, maintain quality, and define its schema.

### 2. Data as a Product
Each domain treats its data as a first-class product with:
- Discoverable (catalog entry)
- Addressable (stable API/URI)
- Understandable (documented schema, SLOs)
- Trustworthy (data quality guarantees)
- Secure (access controls)

### 3. Self-Serve Data Infrastructure
A platform team provides tools so domain teams can build and serve data products without needing platform expertise — data storage, pipeline tooling, catalog, observability.

### 4. Federated Computational Governance
Central governance sets global standards (security, privacy, quality thresholds). Domains implement them. No central bottleneck for every decision.

sections.title: Data Product Structure
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph Orders Data Product
        Raw[Raw Events\nKafka] --> Transform[Transform\nSpark/Flink]
        Transform --> Serve[Serve\nAPI / Parquet / SQL view]
        Serve --> Contract[Data Contract\nSchema + SLOs]
        Contract --> Catalog[Data Catalog\nBackstage / DataHub]
    end
    Consumer1[BI Team] -->|query| Serve
    Consumer2[ML Team] -->|query| Serve
    Consumer3[Finance] -->|query| Serve
    style Contract fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Catalog fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Data Contracts

A data contract is a formal agreement between data producers and consumers defining schema, SLOs, and semantics:

```yaml
# orders-data-contract.yaml
name: orders-completed
version: 2.1.0
owner: team-orders

schema:
  type: record
  fields:
    - name: order_id
      type: string
      description: Unique order identifier
    - name: customer_id
      type: string
    - name: total_amount
      type: decimal
    - name: currency
      type: string
      enum: [USD, EUR, GBP]
    - name: completed_at
      type: timestamp

slos:
  freshness: < 5 minutes
  completeness: > 99.9%
  availability: > 99.5%

access:
  classification: internal
  pii_fields: [customer_id]
  requires_approval: false
```

sections.question: What does 'Data as a Product' mean in Data Mesh?
options: Data should be sold to external customers as a revenue stream
Domain teams treat their data outputs as first-class products with discoverability, SLOs, documentation, and ownership accountability
All data must go through a product management review process
Data products are stored in a product database rather than a data warehouse
sections.explanation: In Data Mesh, 'Data as a Product' means applying product thinking to data. Domain teams publish data products with proper documentation, stable schemas (versioned contracts), quality SLOs, discoverability in a catalog, and accountable ownership — just like a software team would for an API product.
